evidence_quotes.quote: shutdown of online video games when companies disable essential online services, rendering the games non-functionaleven for paying customers.
These games are often sold as products with a so-called lifetime license, yet that license becomes meaningless when the software no longer works.

paragraphs.text: A growing issue in consumer protection in the digital market is the shutdown of online video games when companies disable essential online services, rendering the games non-functionaleven for paying customers. This typically happens when a game no longer generates enough profit to justify maintaining its services. While that may be understandable from a business standpoint, it leaves consumers with broken products, no recourse, and no refunds. Customers are never informed at the time of purchase how long a game will remain functional. These games are often sold as products with a so-called lifetime license, yet that license becomes meaningless when the software no longer works. To date, 389 games have been identified as at-risk due to lacking an official end-of-life plan, and 358 have become completely unplayable. This issue extends beyond video games: smart home devices and other digital products can similarly lose essential functions once cloud support is withdrawn. A fair and balanced solution would be to require companies to provide end-of-life plans, including updates to remove unnecessary online dependencies and preserve functionality. The recent EU Citizen Initiative "Stop Killing Games", which reached way over 1 Million signatures, also insists on creating similar protection to consumers. A similar problem arises with games that include licensed contentsuch as musicwhen those licenses expire. Rather than addressing the issue for future sales only, companies often remove the content for all users, altering the product for both existing and new customers alike. This retroactive modification can devalue the original purchase and misrepresent the product consumers initially paid for. A well-known example of this is the repeated removal of songs from various entries in the Grand Theft Auto series. Another serious concern is inability and removal of legal adult-themed video games and content. Consumers often find themselves unable to make purchases due to restrictions imposed by payment processors, even when the content complies with all applicable laws and platform policies. Recently, popular digital game stores like Steam and Itch.io were pressured to delist adult games after U.S.-based credit card companies threatened to stop processing paymentsjeopardizing the viability of these platforms. Japanese businesses have faced similar obstacles in the past when working with Western payment providers. These actions amount to indirect censorship of lawful artistic expression, with financial institutions acting as de facto gatekeepers over what kind of content is allowed in the digital marketplace.
